parentQuestion: A 68-year-old diabetic patient steps on a tack but doesn't notice until she sees blood on the floor. She can still feel light touch on her feet with a cotton swab. Which sensory pathway has likely failed?

options: No, diabetes only affects blood sugar
Yes, high blood sugar damages nerves (diabetic neuropathy)
Only children get nerve damage from diabetes
scaffolds.explain: High blood sugar DAMAGES nerves, especially in the feet and hands. This is called DIABETIC NEUROPATHY — a common and serious complication.

wrongExplains.0: Diabetes affects many systems. Nerve damage is a major complication.
wrongExplains.2: Diabetic neuropathy occurs at any age, especially in long-standing diabetes.
wrongExplains.3: Diabetes damages nerves; it doesn't help them.

scaffolds.q: Are pain and light touch carried by the same nerve fibers?
options: Yes, all sensations use identical fibers
No, different fiber types carry different sensations
scaffolds.explain: Pain travels on SMALL, unmyelinated fibers (C and Aδ). Light touch travels on LARGER, myelinated fibers (Aβ). Different fibers, different vulnerabilities.

wrongExplains.0: Different sensations use different fiber types — that's why they can be selectively lost.
wrongExplains.2: Touch definitely uses nerve fibers. All sensations require neural transmission.
wrongExplains.3: Pain definitely exists and uses specific nerve fibers.

scaffolds.q: Which nerve fibers are more vulnerable to diabetic damage?
options: Small fibers are more vulnerable and damaged first
scaffolds.explain: SMALL FIBERS have higher metabolic demands and are damaged FIRST. Pain and temperature sensation disappear BEFORE light touch.

wrongExplains.0: Large fibers are actually more resilient. Small fibers die first.
wrongExplains.2: Fibers have different vulnerabilities. Small fibers are affected earlier.
wrongExplains.3: Diabetes definitely damages nerve fibers, especially small ones.

scaffolds.q: What type of nerve fibers do nociceptors use?
options: Small nerve fibers (unmyelinated C and thin Aδ)
scaffolds.explain: Pain signals travel on UNMYELINATED C fibers and thinly myelinated Aδ fibers. These SMALL FIBERS are fragile and die first in diabetes.

wrongExplains.0: Large myelinated fibers carry touch. Pain uses small fibers.
wrongExplains.2: Nociceptors definitely use nerve fibers to transmit pain signals.
wrongExplains.3: Motor fibers carry movement commands. Nociceptors are sensory.

scaffolds.q: What type of fibers do light touch receptors use?
scaffolds.explain: Light touch (like Meissner's corpuscles) travels on thick, well-MYELINATED Aβ fibers. These SURVIVE LONGER in diabetic neuropathy.

wrongExplains.0: Small unmyelinated fibers carry pain. Light touch uses larger fibers.
wrongExplains.2: Touch receptors definitely use nerve fibers.
wrongExplains.3: Touch uses distinct fibers from pain. They're separate pathways.

scaffolds.q: Can a patient lose pain sensation while keeping light touch?
options: No, you always lose both together
Yes, if only the small fibers are damaged
You can only lose touch, not pain
scaffolds.explain: This is called DISSOCIATED SENSORY LOSS. Small fibers die (no pain), large fibers survive (touch preserved). Different fibers, different fates.

wrongExplains.0: Because fibers are separate, they can be selectively damaged.
wrongExplains.2: Pain can definitely be lost while touch remains — that's this patient's situation.
wrongExplains.3: Both can be lost, but they can also be selectively affected.

scaffolds.q: What happened to this diabetic patient's sensation?
options: She lost pain but kept touch (small fiber damage)
She lost touch but kept pain
Nothing is wrong with her sensation
scaffolds.explain: She can feel the cotton swab (LIGHT TOUCH INTACT) but not the tack (PAIN LOST). Small fibers died, large fibers survived.

wrongExplains.0: She can still feel light touch. Only pain is lost.
wrongExplains.2: It's the opposite — she kept touch but lost pain.
wrongExplains.3: Something is definitely wrong — she couldn't feel a tack!

scaffolds.q: Which receptors have likely failed in this patient?
scaffolds.explain: FREE NERVE ENDINGS (nociceptors) have failed. The small fibers carrying pain signals are damaged. Large fibers carrying touch are intact.

wrongExplains.0: Meissner's are working — she can feel the cotton swab.
wrongExplains.2: Photoreceptors are in the eyes. This is about foot sensation.
wrongExplains.3: Auditory receptors are in the ears. This is about foot sensation.

scaffolds.q: The sensory pathway that has failed is carried by which receptors?
scaffolds.explain: FREE NERVE ENDINGS (nociceptors)! These detect pain and travel on small, vulnerable fibers. Diabetic neuropathy killed her pain sensation while sparing touch.

wrongExplains.0: Meissner's corpuscles (touch) are working — she feels the cotton swab.
wrongExplains.2: Pacinian corpuscles detect vibration, not specifically pain.
wrongExplains.3: Muscle spindles detect position, not pain or touch.
